query: What are CSS media queries and how should you use them in production?

competitors.label: Using media queries
competitors.selectionReason: This is MDN's canonical guide for media query syntax, conditions, operators, and media features.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on media query syntax, condition composition, and the available media features.
theirGaps: The guide is less interview-shaped around breakpoint strategy, overfitting pitfalls, and when viewport queries should give way to container queries.

realWorldUseCases: Media queries are not just responsive CSS trivia. In production they define your breakpoint strategy, user-preference handling, and fallback rules when layouts change across devices.
followUpConfusion:
Advanced Features
  • Media queries support prefers-color-scheme for dark/light mode.
  • prefers-reduced-motion helps accessibility by disabling animations for users who prefer reduced motion.
  • Container queries (in modern browsers) extend this concept to parent element widths rather than the viewport.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains how to write and combine media queries, but it is less focused on one production breakpoint strategy and the pitfalls of viewport-only thinking.
actionableExamples: It includes direct syntax examples and feature combinations, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: The guide covers media features well, but our page is more explicit about user-preference queries and the viewport-versus-container follow-up.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter component-level example that contrasts viewport media queries with a container-query handoff.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical syntax reference, but our page is more useful as an interview answer because it turns media queries into a production breakpoint strategy question and explicitly raises user preferences and container-query follow-ups.

competitors.label: CSS Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's CSS interview bank is directly relevant and explicitly includes responsive design and media-query-related CSS fundamentals.
theirStrengths: It is a strong CSS interview-prep hub with responsive design, layout, and selector coverage.
theirGaps: The landing page is broad and not dedicated to media-query strategy or viewport-versus-container trade-offs.

realWorldUseCases: Strong answers talk about mobile-first defaults, pitfalls like overfitting to a few screen widths, and when viewport queries should give way to container queries.
followUpConfusion: Modern best practice is to start with default (mobile) styles and add media queries for larger viewports.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd covers responsive CSS topics broadly, but the hub page is less explicit about a breakpoint strategy and about user-preference media features.
actionableExamples: Because the bank provides quick answers and detailed explanations, the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: The hub is less explicit about container queries as the likely follow-up when viewport breakpoints become too blunt.

actionableExamples: Keep one shorter mobile-first snippet where the default styles stay untouched and only the larger breakpoint adds layout changes.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is helpful as a broad CSS interview resource, but our page is more focused on the exact production strategy and follow-up confusion around modern media-query usage.

competitors.label: Media queries
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev has a direct media-queries guide and is the strongest tutorial/reference competitor for responsive design strategy in this cluster.
theirStrengths: web.dev is strong on modern responsive-design guidance and performance-minded layout decisions.
theirGaps: The guide is less compact as a single interview answer about breakpoint strategy, user preferences, and container-query handoff.

realWorldUseCases: In production they define your breakpoint strategy, user-preference handling, and fallback rules when layouts change across devices.
followUpConfusion: Container queries (in modern browsers) extend this concept to parent element widths rather than the viewport.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev covers responsive layout strategy well, but our page is more direct about overfitting breakpoints and about using media queries as a production decision framework.
actionableExamples: Its examples are strong and practical, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: The guide is less explicitly framed around the interview follow-up of when media queries stop being enough and container queries should take over.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter example that combines prefers-reduced-motion with a layout breakpoint so syntax and strategy stay together.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is a strong modern responsive-design reference, but our page is more compact and more explicit about the key follow-up decision points interviewers expect: mobile-first defaults, preference queries, and container-query handoff.
